text: Phenomenologist Edmund Husserl described consciousness as having a temporal structure: each moment contains a 'retention' of the just-past and a 'protention' reaching toward the imminent future. This threefold structure — retention, primal impression, protention — is what makes experience feel continuous rather than a series of disconnected snapshots. Constant digital interruption disrupts this temporal weave. When a notification breaks a train of thought, it is not just a pause; it severs the retention-protention arc, collapsing the experiential horizon. Recovery is not instantaneous — researchers estimate it takes over 20 minutes to fully reconstitute deep attention after an interruption.
question: According to the passage, what is Husserl's 'protention'?

options.text: The vivid memory of a completed experience stored in long-term memory.
A forward-reaching dimension of consciousness oriented toward the imminent future.
The brain's mechanism for suppressing irrelevant sensory input during focus.
A retrospective reconstruction that gives experience an illusion of continuity.
